text: Not all attention systems are hijackable in the same way. Psychologist Michael Posner mapped three largely independent attention networks: alerting, which detects sudden change; orienting, which selects among sensory inputs; and executive control, which resolves conflict and maintains goals. Variable-reinforcement feeds target the alerting network most directly — novelty and unpredictability are exactly the signals it evolved to detect. Executive control, seated in the prefrontal cortex, can in principle override this — but it is metabolically expensive and degrades under fatigue or cognitive load. The asymmetry is critical: the alerting network operates automatically; executive control requires effort. Platforms are designed to exhaust the latter while perpetually stimulating the former.
question: Why is the alerting attention network more vulnerable to variable-reinforcement feeds than executive control?

options.text: The alerting network processes information faster and is therefore harder to suppress consciously
Executive control degrades under fatigue while the alerting network operates automatically
The alerting network receives direct dopamine input; executive control does not
Executive control is active only during explicit decision-making tasks, not passive browsing
